返回本批次

为什么当你过着有意义的日子时，它们看起来毫无意义

Why meaningful days look like nothing while you are living them

pilgrima.ge 作者 momentmaker 分数 30 评论 16 2026/04/12 11:04
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 那天是阴天，和你记忆中的不太一样。那种不威胁下雨、不许出太阳、在你看着它时不会改变的阴天。灰色就是天气。周二的福冈。你坐火车。你走得很远。快到终点线了。你在一个你不会记得名字的车站下车，在一个看起来像大多数社区的街区——一间低矮混凝土砌块的浴室，一个游乐场，里面涂着原色的金属设备，经过十年的风吹雨打已经变软了，广场后面有一个小开放广场，穿着运动服的老人正在互相扔球，他们的专注程度看起来很像祈祷。广场中央站着一座青铜雕像，仿佛他一直都在那里。三把剑的剑客。索隆。一个女孩正在和他一起自拍。她的年龄不可能超过二十岁。她把手机放在一臂远的地方，倾斜它以确保角度合适，你在礼貌的距离外等待，当她放下手机时，你走上前，用蹩脚的日语提出——用一个学过“照片”和“在一起”这个词但很快就放弃了的人的蹩脚日语——帮她接电话。她答应了。你拿去吧。她回了一句，这就是一个提议：她会接受你们中的一个。在这里，记忆变薄了。您不记得是否接受了。您不记得照片是否存在。十年后，你将不知道陌生人的相机胶卷中是否有这样一张照片：在一个只有阴天的日子里，在火车线路尽头的操场上，一个外国人站在青铜剑客旁边。不确定性本身就是让记忆感觉真实的一部分。你当时就在那里。其他细节——那些在不同类型的故事中很重要的细节——拒绝留下来。你站了一分钟。你看着雕像。你看着老人扔球。然后你走回车站，继续前行。 下午剩下的时间，这个命令并不是刻意的。手机上的地图、雕像之间绘制的路径、火车和公共汽车的任何组合所选择的路线都将覆盖地面而无需折返。当可以走路的时候你就走了。你乘公共汽车的时候不是。乔巴在动物园旁边。一个儿童大小的驯鹿医生，用青铜铸成，放置在动物围栏边缘的矮墙附近。进出动物园的人们从他身边经过，没有放慢速度，就像人们经过邮箱一样。 路飞在一座大楼的庭院里，过了一会儿，你认出这是一座市政办公室——有点像市政厅，又像档案馆。青铜色的草帽少年，一只手臂高举，站在大人去办理登记的地方外。这样的对比并没有让人觉得不尊重。也没有让人感到敬畏的感觉。感觉就像青铜器被放置在日本小镇上放置青铜器的地方：那里有一些露天混凝土和一些人行交通，没有理由不这样做。当距离允许时，你可以在车站之间步行。当火车没有出现时，你又坐了火车。你没有做笔记。下午就这样愉快、低调地过去了。当你是一个无所事事的外国人，对一个你不了解的城镇时，一个下午就这样过去了。然后这句话——直白地说出来，没有夸大其词：这并不傻。甚至连恭敬都没有。这只是一件事。你坐火车回到福冈。你吃了东西。你睡了。早上灰色的天空变成了另外一种灰色的天空，如果有人问你前一天做了什么，你会说：我在熊本散步。有雕像。很好。你不会说朝圣。你不会想到这个词。你并没有试图被感动。你没有被感动。照片这次步行是有原因的，但这个原因很小，以至于回想起来才看起来像是一个原因。几周前，在东京，你遇到了一位年轻女子。这次会议并不重要——或者说很重要，但不是本文所讨论的方式。称她为你曾经交谈过一次，然后就不再交谈的朋友，这种会面在日历上没有留下任何痕迹，但在神经系统中留下了一些东西，在之后的几天里一直在嗡嗡作响。在你们空闲的时间里的某个时刻，她拿出手机给你看了一张照片。那是索隆的照片。那个在操场上，在火车线的尽头，在她长大的小镇上。她没有说你应该去。她没有说那是我的家乡，我想念它。她没有说任何你能记得清楚的话。她给你看了照片。仅此而已。一些碎片仍然存在。有些残留在体内，就像种子埋在人行道的裂缝中一样——未经你的同意，当时也没有引起任何注意。她给你看了一张照片，几周后，出于一些看似不像的原因，你买了一张火车票。这就是朝圣的真正开始。不是通过电话。没有远见。不是夜里有一个声音。别人提供的一个片段却忘记了他们提供的。朝圣者并不总是知道自己是朝圣者。有时他们只是一个放不下照片的人。蘑菇月过去了。有多少并不重要。足以让熊本悄悄地进入你曾经度过的愉快的一天，再也不会了。你现在在一个不同的国家，在一个安静的房间里，看电视。这是 Netflix 真人改编的《海贼王》第二季。你一直想抽出时间去做这件事。你年轻时就开始看动画，看了大概一百集，就渐渐离开了，让故事在没有你的情况下继续十五年。漫画现在已经过了一千​​章了。这部动画在目前的一些观众出生之前就已经开始播放了。真人秀正在把人们拉回来——像你这样的人，早早退出的人，从未完成的人，从未开始的人。一群漂流类的观众回到了他们放错地方的故事，并且不知道自己失踪了。你还拿走了少量的蘑菇。不是仪式。对化学也没有什么敬畏之心。周六，一间安静的公寓，车身设置得比平时稍宽。屏幕上的船员正在驶入伟大航路。你已经有十五年没有想过伟大航路了。如果你必须向一个没有为这个故事付出几十年生命的人解释的话，伟大航路就是海贼王世界中间的海洋——一片没有地图的、不可预测的、危险的海域，每一个想要宝藏的海盗都必须去那里。这份宝藏被称为海贼王。这是前任海贼王留下的。没有人知道它是什么。从来没有人找到过它。整个故事——一千个章节，二十八年的每周出版——就是关于航行在海洋中寻找那个东西。在胸部的某个地方——是的，化学反应正在发挥作用，但化学反应只是打开一扇已经存在的门——你立刻明白了：海贼王是空的。盒子里什么也没有。洋葱被剥到了中心，中心什么也没有。整个一千章的旅程是一个精心设计的、欢乐的、宇宙级的笑话——一个跨越一代人整个阅读一生的故事，其妙语是旅程一直是宝藏。船员们。伤疤。笑声。中间——将船上的陌生人变成亲人的纽带。伟大航路本身。你以为你在读漫画。漫画在读你。现代经文滚动。公寓保持安静。在接下来的半个小时里，第二件事开始浮出水面。海贼王不是一本书。这不是一部电影。这不是一个周末狂欢的系列，然后紧接着另一个系列。这是一本可以陪伴一个人度过整个成年生活的文本。漫画于1997年开始创作。第一年接触它的一个十岁的孩子现在已经快四十岁了。他们跟随剧组经历了第一次心碎、职业选择、结婚、离婚、父母去世、孩子出生。在成为自我的每个阶段，文本都存在于他们的心灵中。无论文本对他们做了什么，它都是在现实生活的实时过程中缓慢地进行的——一章一章，一周又一周。这曾经是经文所做的。《薄伽梵歌》有七百节经文。 《法句经》是一个下午的读物。如果你自律的话，一年之内就能读完《圣经》。这些文本从来不短，因为短在精神上是优越的。它们很短，因为在持有它们的文化中，人们被期望在数十年的时间里一次又一次地回到同一个小文本。文本是相同的。读者不是。这就是沉思传统在谈论螺旋时的含义——回到同一点，但在不同的高度。海贼王以相反的方向解决了同样的问题。不是多次重复的短文本，而是以现实生活的速度展开的长文本。你不能走捷径。你不能浏览它。你可能会偏离它——大多数人都会这样做——但偏离本身就会成为文本的一部分。你退出的那一集和真人叫你回来的那一集之间的间隙是故事对你所做的事情的一部分。长度就是练习。我不是来告诉你漫画是神圣的文本。我告诉你，没有古老神圣文本的一代人偶然发现了一个足够长的故事，可以做圣经曾经做过的事情——缓慢地展开，同一位读者在不同的高度返回，拒绝一次性交出所有的意义——并且无论他们是否知道，都得到了回报，以他们的文化允许他们接受的唯一形式的圣经。你不必相信这一点。你只需要注意到，当船员们驶入 Netflix 改编的伟大航路时，一个已经从伟大航路漂流出来的成年人十五年的故事让人感觉有一些娱乐无法传递的东西到达了胸口——有一些东西正在得到满足。而与他们相遇的事情也并非凭空而来。它来自于一个故事的重量，这个故事在他们的大部分时间里都在他们的休息中。重量到达这里就是这篇文章要说的事情，在熊本度过了一个阴天，一个星期六的蘑菇和十五年的漂流才到达它。熊本步行是一次朝圣。不是一个比喻。不像朝圣。一次朝圣。一个外国人跟着一张陌生人给他看的照片来到了一个他没有其他理由去参观的小镇，并在虔诚的车站之间走了一条刻意的路线，寻找与他从小就带着的故事有关的物品，这些物品的发现完成了他不知道是开放的神经系统中的一个小回路。车站是卡通海盗的青铜雕像这一事实并不重要。事实上，为这次步行播种的女人不是女祭司，而是他曾经见过的人，这一事实并不重要。没有仪式、没有祈祷、没有向导、没有证书，这一事实并不重要。这是一次朝圣，因为这座建筑就是一次朝圣。出发、意图、停留、见证、返回。但这里是几乎所有朝圣工作框架都会出错的部分：步行，当你行走时，几乎从来没有感觉像朝圣。感觉就像一天。感觉就像一个阴天的下午，有一些火车和一些雕像，还有一位老人在安静的广场上扔球。感觉上，使用实际上诚实、不愚蠢、甚至不虔诚的词语，只是一件事情。从卡米诺回来并告诉你燃烧的每一公里都充满意义的作家们并没有撒谎，确切地说，他们告诉你的是后来组装起来的版本——为出版而编辑的版本，其中重量已经被分配回它现在所属的日子的版本。此刻，大多数日子只是几天。体重过后。有时是一个月后。有时一年。有时，十五年之后，一小剂量的蘑菇和Netflix节目中的一个场景，一群虚构的海盗进入未绘制地图的海洋，你在熊本所做的事情会立刻回到你的脑海中，在你做这件事时不存在的光芒中被照亮。这就是老老师说你无法区分傻瓜和圣人时的意思。傻瓜之所以做一件事，是因为他没有想到不这样做。圣人之所以做一件事，是因为他已经克服了一切不该做的理由，并从另一面走了出来。从外面看，它们看起来是一样的。从内部来看，一个是无意识的，一个是清醒的——从第一个到第二个的唯一方法是在它们之间走长长的螺旋，而当你这样做时，几乎没有任何行走表明它是什么。你从路上走开。你过着一种生活。路一直走着你。有一天，在桑拿房里，在厨房里，在一个正在播放 Netflix 节目的黑暗房间里，路从另一扇门回来，你看到了它。路在你身边走，回到操场。阴天。青铜剑士手持三把剑。那个二十岁的女孩把手机放在一臂之遥。安静的广场上，穿着运动服的老人互相扔球。火车站位于火车线路的尽头，外国人站在那里，没有什么可申报的，也没有理由来到那个城镇，除了一张他看过的照片，他无法完全忘记。那一刻没有任何改变。光是同样的光。青铜没有动。老人们还在扔球——或者他们已经扔完球回家了，等死；但在你现在看到的那一刻，他们仍然在扔掉它，因为那一刻被保存在奇怪的永恒的金库中，那里保存着未被认可的朝圣之旅，直到有人记得认出它们。改变的是你现在知道了——坐在十五年后的一个大陆的公寓里，片尾字幕滚动着，越年轻的你会被称为书呆子，越老的你最终会明白是一段经文——阴天也算数。这是唯一的词。计数。因为有人给你看了一张照片，你所走了的步数。那些在外国城市里闲逛的下午，没有论文，也没有其他目的地，但了解一个城镇是件好事。你和一个故事一起度过的那些年，你最终逐渐远离了它。这本书你二十岁读了一半，三十五岁就无法停止思考。那些不为人知的平凡日子，因此你没有认真对待。它们很重要。他们正在数数。其中一些仍在计算，因为其中一些尚未解决，并且要再过一年或十年才能解决，或者直到正确的节目在正确的月份播出并且你体内产生正确的化学反应，然后计数就会结束，体重就会落地，那一天你已经忘记的那一天将会是一切开始的那一天。这是真正的宇宙喜剧——老老师们所暗示的喜剧，也是漫画二十八年来在每一章的边缘绘制的喜剧。尽管宝藏确实存在，但并不是说宝藏一直就在你体内。珍贵的是，你走过的那条路还在走着。你迈出的每一步都是因为你不知道还能做什么。每次绕道进入火车线路尽头的游乐场。每一张照片，陌生人给你看过一次，然后就忘记了她给你看过。所有这一切都是你不同意、无法拒绝、也不会完全理解的步行的一部分，直到你不寻找的门在未来的某个房间打开。朝圣者并不总是知道他们是朝圣者。有时他们必须通过自己的生活来告诉他们，后来，在重量决定到达的那一刻。你的正在到达。来自道路我在上一篇文章结束后的一周写了这篇文章——那篇关于没有选择道路的朝圣者。这是为那些确实选择了它然后忘记了的朝圣者，或者那些选择了它但不知道自己已经选择了它的朝圣者，或者是几年后即将发现的朝圣者，他们依稀记得的一个阴天的下午是这一切开始的那一天。如果这一切都处于适当的沉默中，请将其转发给您在某个车站曾经见过的朋友。他们已经知道了。——朝圣者时代

Original Summary

  1. The day was cloudy, and not in a way you would remember. The kind of cloudy that does not threaten rain, does not promise sun, does not change while you are looking at it. A gray that is simply the weather. Fukuoka on a Tuesday. You take the train. You take it a long way. Almost to the end of the line.You get off at a station you will not remember the name of, in a neighborhood that looks like most neighborhoods — a bathroom in a low concrete block, a playground with metal equipment painted in primary colors that have softened under a decade of weather, and beyond it a small open square where elderly people in tracksuits are throwing a ball to one another with a kind of attention that looks a great deal like prayer.And standing in the middle of it, as though he had always been there, is a bronze statue of a swordsman with three swords.Zoro.A girl is taking a selfie with him. She cannot be older than twenty. She has the phone at arm's length and is tilting it to get the angle right, and you wait a polite distance away, and when she lowers the phone you step forward and offer — in the broken Japanese of someone who learned the word for photo and the word for together and gave up shortly after — to take it for her.She says yes. You take it. She says something back, and the something is an offer: she will take one of you. And here the memory thins. You do not remember if you accepted. You do not remember whether the photograph exists. A decade from now, you will not know whether there is a picture somewhere in a stranger's camera roll of a foreigner standing next to a bronze swordsman at a playground at the end of a train line on a day that was only cloudy. The uncertainty itself is part of what makes the memory feel true. You were there. The other details — the ones that would matter in a different kind of story — refused to stay.You stood for a minute. You looked at the statue. You watched the elderly people throw their ball. Then you walked back to the station and you kept going.The Rest of the AfternoonThe order was not deliberate. A map on a phone, a path drawn between statues, the route chosen by whatever combination of trains and buses would cover the ground without doubling back. You walked when it was possible to walk. You took transit when it was not.Chopper was by the zoo. A reindeer-doctor the size of a child, cast in bronze, set near a low wall at the edge of the animal enclosures. People went in and out of the zoo past him without slowing, the way people pass a mailbox.Luffy was in the courtyard of a building you recognized, after a moment, as a municipal office — something like a city hall, something like a records building. A straw-hatted boy in bronze, one arm raised, outside a place where adults went to renew registrations.The contrast did not feel disrespectful. It did not feel reverent either. It felt like the bronze had been placed where bronze is placed in small Japanese towns: where there is a bit of open concrete and some human foot traffic and no reason not to.You walked between stops when the distance allowed. You rode the train again when it did not. You did not take notes. The afternoon passed in the pleasant, low-key way an afternoon passes when you are a foreigner with nothing to do and a town you do not know.And then this — spoken plainly, not inflated:It was not silly. It was not even reverent. It was just a thing.You rode the train back to Fukuoka. You ate something. You slept. In the morning the gray sky had become a different gray sky, and if someone had asked you what you had done the day before, you would have said: I walked around Kumamoto. There were statues. It was nice. You would not have said pilgrimage. The word would not have occurred to you. You were not trying to be moved.You were not moved.The PhotographThe walk had a cause, but the cause was so small that it only looks like a cause in retrospect.A few weeks earlier, in Tokyo, you had met a young woman. The meeting does not matter — or it matters, but not in a way that belongs in this essay. Call her a friend you spoke with once and then did not speak with again, the kind of meeting that leaves no track in a calendar but leaves something in the nervous system that keeps humming for days afterward. At some point in the hours you had, she took out her phone and showed you a picture.It was a picture of Zoro. The one in the playground, at the end of the train line, in the town where she grew up.She did not say you should go. She did not say it is my hometown and I miss it. She did not say anything you can remember clearly. She showed you the picture. That was all.Some fragments stay. Some lodge in the body the way a seed lodges in a crack in the pavement — without your consent, without any attention being paid to it at the time. She showed you a picture, and weeks later, for reasons that did not feel like reasons, you bought a train ticket.This is how pilgrimages actually begin. Not with a call. Not with a vision. Not with a voice in the night. With a fragment someone else offers and forgets they offered. The pilgrim does not always know they are a pilgrim. Sometimes they are just a person who cannot let go of a photograph.The MushroomMonths pass. How many is not important. Enough for Kumamoto to have slid quietly into the category of a pleasant day you once had and no further.You are in a different country now, in a quiet room, watching television. It is the second season of the One Piece live action adaptation on Netflix. You had been meaning to get around to it. You had started the anime as a younger person, watched maybe a hundred episodes, drifted away, let the story continue without you for another fifteen years.The manga is now past its thousandth chapter. The anime has been running since before some of its current viewers were born. The live action is pulling people back — people like you, people who quit early, people who never finished, people who never started. A whole drift-class of viewers returning to a story they had misplaced and did not know they were missing.You have also taken a small amount of a mushroom. Not a ceremony. Nothing reverent about the chemistry either. A Saturday, a quiet apartment, a body set to slightly wider than usual.The crew on the screen is sailing into the Grand Line. You have not thought about the Grand Line in fifteen years.If you had to explain it to someone who has not given decades of their life to the story, the Grand Line is the ocean in the middle of the world of One Piece — the unmapped, unpredictable, dangerous stretch of sea where every pirate who wants the treasure has to go. The treasure is called the One Piece. It was left there by the previous Pirate King. Nobody knows what it is. Nobody has ever found it. The entire story — a thousand chapters, twenty-eight years of weekly publication — is about sailing that ocean to find that thing.And somewhere in the chest — and yes, the chemistry is helping, but the chemistry is only opening a door that was already there — you understand all at once:The One Piece is empty. There is nothing in the box. The onion has been peeled to its center, and the center is no-thing. The whole thousand-chapter voyage has been an elaborate, joyful, cosmic-scale joke — a story told across a generation's entire reading lifetime, whose punchline is that the journey was the treasure all along. The crew. The scars. The laughter. The nakama — the bond that turns strangers on a ship into kin. The Grand Line itself.You thought you were reading a manga.The manga was reading you.Modern ScriptureThe credits roll. The apartment stays quiet. In the half-hour that follows, a second thing begins to surface.One Piece is not a book. It is not a film. It is not a series one binges on a weekend and then files away next to the other series. It is a text a person can spend their entire adult life with.The manga began in 1997. A ten-year-old who picked it up in its first year is nearly forty now. They have followed the crew through first heartbreak, career choice, marriage, divorce, the death of parents, the birth of children. The text has been in their psyche through every stage of becoming a self. And whatever the text has been doing to them, it has been doing slowly — chapter by chapter, week by week, in the real time of an actual life.This used to be what scripture did.The Gita is seven hundred verses. The Dhammapada is a single afternoon's reading. The Bible you can get through in a year if you are disciplined. These texts were never short because shortness was spiritually superior. They were short because in the cultures that held them, a person was expected to return to the same small text again and again, across decades.The text is the same. The reader is not. This is what the contemplative traditions mean when they talk about the spiral — the return to the same point, but at a different elevation.One Piece solves the same problem in the opposite direction. Not a short text returned to many times, but a long text that unfolds at the speed of an actual life. You cannot shortcut it. You cannot skim it. You can drift from it — most people do — but the drift itself becomes part of the text. The gap between the episode where you quit and the episode where the live action calls you back is part of what the story is doing to you. The length is the practice.I am not here to tell you that a manga is a sacred text. I am telling you that a generation without the old sacred texts has found, by accident, a story long enough to do what scripture once did — the slow unfolding, the return of the same reader at different elevations, the refusal to hand over the meaning all at once — and has been rewarded, whether they knew it or not, with a scripture in the only form their culture would let them receive one.You do not have to believe this. You only have to notice that when the crew sails into the Grand Line in a Netflix adaptation and a grown adult who has drifted from the story for fifteen years feels something arrive in the chest that entertainment cannot deliver — something is being met. And the thing meeting them did not come from nowhere. It came from the weight of a story that has been sitting in them, at rest, for most of their life.The Weight Arrives AfterHere is the thing the essay came to say, and it took a cloudy day in Kumamoto and a Saturday on mushrooms and a fifteen-year drift to get to it.The Kumamoto walk was a pilgrimage. Not a metaphor for one. Not like a pilgrimage. A pilgrimage.A foreigner followed a photograph a stranger had shown him to a town he had no other reason to visit, and walked a deliberate route between stations of devotion to find objects that mattered to a story he had carried since he was a child, and the finding of them completed a small circuit in the nervous system he did not know was open.The fact that the stations were bronze statues of cartoon pirates did not matter. The fact that the woman who seeded the walk was not a priestess but someone he had met once did not matter. The fact that there was no ritual, no prayer, no guide, no credential did not matter.It was a pilgrimage because the structure was a pilgrimage. Departure, intention, stations, witness, return.But here is the part that almost every working framework of pilgrimage gets wrong:The walk, while you are walking it, almost never feels like a pilgrimage.It feels like a day. It feels like a cloudy afternoon with some trains and some statues and an elderly person throwing a ball in a quiet square. It feels, to use the words that are actually honest, not silly and not even reverent and just a thing.The writers who return from the Camino and tell you every kilometer burned with meaning are not lying, exactly, but they are telling you the version that assembled itself after — the version edited for publication, the version in which the weight has already been distributed back across the days it now belongs to. In the moment, most of the days were just days.The weight arrives after.Sometimes the after is a month. Sometimes a year. Sometimes it is fifteen years and a small dose of a mushroom and a scene in a Netflix show where a crew of fictional pirates enters the unmapped sea, and the thing you did in Kumamoto comes back to you all at once, lit up in a light that was not there when you were doing it.This is what the old teachers meant when they said you cannot tell the fool from the sage. The fool does a thing because it does not occur to him not to. The sage does a thing because he has passed through every reason not to and come out the other side.From the outside they look identical. From the inside, one is unconscious and one is awake — and the only way to get from the first to the second is to walk the long spiral between them, and almost none of the walking, while you are doing it, announces what it is.You walk off from the road. You live a life. The road keeps walking you.And one day — in a sauna, in a kitchen, in a dark room with a Netflix show playing — the road comes back through a different door, and you see it.The Road Walks YouCome back to the playground.The cloudy day. The bronze swordsman with his three swords. The girl at twenty with her phone at arm's length. The elderly people in tracksuits throwing their ball to one another in the quiet square. The station at the end of a train line, and the foreigner standing there with nothing to declare and no reason to be in that town except a photograph he had been shown and could not quite forget.Nothing has changed about that moment. The light is the same light. The bronze has not moved. The elderly people are still throwing their ball — or they have finished, gone home, left to die; but in the moment you are looking at now, they are still throwing it, because the moment is held in the strange timeless vault where unrecognized pilgrimages are kept until someone remembers to recognize them.What has changed is that you now know — sitting in an apartment a continent and fifteen years away, with the credits rolling on a show the younger you would have called nerdy and the older you has finally understood is a scripture — that the cloudy day counted.That is the only word for it. Counted.The walks you took because someone showed you a photograph. The afternoons you spent wandering foreign cities with no thesis and no destination beyond it is nice to get to know a town. The years you spent with a story you eventually drifted away from. The book you half-read at twenty and cannot stop thinking about at thirty-five. The ordinary days that did not announce themselves and that you therefore did not take seriously.They counted. They are counting. Some of them are still counting, because some of them have not yet resolved, and will not resolve for another year or another decade or until the right show airs in the right month with the right chemistry in your body, and then the counting will finish and the weight will land and the day you had forgotten will turn out to have been the day everything began.This is the real cosmic comedy — the one the old teachers were hinting at, and the one the manga has been drawing in the margins of every chapter for twenty-eight years. Not that the treasure was inside you all along, though it was. The treasure is that the road you walked off of is still walking you.Every step you took because you did not know what else to do. Every detour into a playground at the end of a train line. Every photograph a stranger showed you once and forgot she had shown you. All of it is part of a walk you did not agree to and cannot refuse and will not fully understand until a door you were not looking for opens in some future room.The pilgrim does not always know they are a pilgrim.Sometimes they have to be told by their own life, later, at the moment the weight decides to arrive.Yours is arriving.From the RoadI wrote this the week after the last essay ended — the one about the pilgrim who did not choose the road. This one is for the pilgrim who did choose it and then forgot, or who chose it and did not know they had, or who is about to find out, years from now, that a cloudy afternoon they half-remember was the day it all began. If this lands in the right kind of silence, forward it to the friend you met once at a station somewhere. They already know.— The Pilgrim Age

评论（默认中文）

thelucent 2026/04/12 13:33
我尝试用Claude Opus 4.6写一篇短篇小说，我给了它大纲和草稿，风格和这篇文章很相似。

我试图让它远离这种写作，因为它感觉很奇怪。但它总是尝试输出类似的东西。或许我只是不习惯看小说。

所以我很好奇，Claude 是用什么样的训练数据来训练的，很难摆脱这种风格。

于是我打开kindle翻看推荐的热门小说。只需阅读其免费样本即可。

它们的相似之处是惊人的。现在，我不知道推荐的小说是训练数据，还是LLM写的。或者也许这就是小说家的写作方式。

我什至尝试从头开始写完整的章节。并要求克劳德用我的写作风格替我代写第二章。它仍然不会遵循我的风格，从文章开始就一直这样写。

并不是指责文章利用LLM代写。即便如此，用LLM代写也可以。这只是我身边的一件轶事，关于法学硕士如何未能遵循我的写作风格并不断回到其培训数据。

I tried writing a short novel using Claude Opus 4.6, I gave it outline and raw draft, and the style is very similar to this writing.

I tried to steer it away from this kind of writing because it feels weird. But it always try to output something similar to this. Or maybe I am just not used to reading novel.

So I was curious, what kind of training data was Claude trained on, that its very hard to steer it out from this style.

So I opened my kindle and looking through the recommended popular novels. Just reading through its free samples.

And the similarities are striking. Now, I dont know whether the recommended novel is the training data, or its actually written by LLM. Or maybe its just how novelist writes.

I even tried writing full chapter from scratch. And asked Claude to ghost write the second chapter for me using my writing style. It still wont follow my style and keeps writing in this kind of style from the article.

Not accusing the article of using an LLM to ghost write. Even so its fine to use LLM to ghost write. Its just one anecdote from my side, on how LLM fails to follow my writing style and keeps coming back to its training data.

在 HN 中查看
computably 2026/04/12 14:06
> 相似之处是惊人的。现在，我不知道推荐的小说是训练数据，还是LLM写的。或者也许这就是小说家的写作方式。

对于传统出版的作品，排除法学硕士撰写的内容很简单，只需查找 2022 年 11 月 30 日之前出版的任何内容即可。

> And the similarities are striking. Now, I dont know whether the recommended novel is the training data, or its actually written by LLM. Or maybe its just how novelist writes.

For traditionally published works, it's trivial to exclude LLM-written content, just look for anything published before Nov 30, 2022.

在 HN 中查看
elcapitan 2026/04/12 14:38
这也是网络搜索的一个很好的过滤器，可以排除大量垃圾结果（如果特定搜索对于非最新结果有意义）
Which is also a good filter for web searches to exclude a lot of garbage results (if the specific search makes sense for non-recent results)
在 HN 中查看
fingerlocks 2026/04/12 14:52
为什么要停止传统出版的作品呢？在互联网消亡之前，几乎所有形式的写作都保证是手工制作的、有机的，并且是用 100% 自然智能制作的。

人造的东西通常有一种奇怪的味道，但天哪，它确实又快又方便。

Why stop with traditionally published works? Before dead-internet-day, very-nearly all forms of writing were guaranteed to be hand crafted, organic, and made with 100% Natural Intelligence.

The artificial stuff often has an odd taste, but boy it sure is quick and convenient.

在 HN 中查看
ares623 2026/04/12 15:17
你开玩笑，但我敢打赌，这个论坛中的每个人，当在充满机器人的论坛和保证只有人类*的论坛之间做出选择时，他们会选择后者。

* 这是一个假设场景。我不知道有任何保证仅限人类的数字论坛。

You joke, but I bet every person in this forum, when presented the choice between a bot-filled forum and a guaranteed human-only* forum, they'd go with the latter.

* this is a hypothetical scenario. I don't know any guaranteed human-only digital forums.

在 HN 中查看
sigbottle 2026/04/12 15:46
在这一点上，我与法学硕士进行了足够多的研究交流，我觉得我有足够好的结构可以让他们跳上跳下到主要来源和东西，所以我不会太容易对他们感到恼火。

然而我已经有超过 15 年没有认真反思过我的社交媒体消费习惯了，而且这些年来我对社交媒体越来越恼火。

并不是有点愤世嫉俗，但我的社交媒体使用存在严重错误，尤其是当我知道另一边有一个真正的人时，再加上对评论者日益增加的烦恼以及我在阅读社交媒体后的感受。

这可能与多巴胺/自助有关，但实际上不是，我认为所有这些都是问题的一部分（在高中起飞时发现的）。随着我的成熟，我与媒体的基本互动方式似乎变得如此可怕和恶心。

I converse enough with LLMs for research at this point where I feel I have a good enough structure to hop on/off them to primary sources and stuff, so I don't get annoyed with them too easily.

Whereas I haven't seriously reflected on my social media consumption habits for over 15 years, and over the years I'm getting more and more annoyed at social media.

Not to be a bit misanthropic, but there's something seriously wrong with my social media usage, especially when I know there's a real human on the other side, combined with ever increasing annoyance towards commenters and just the feelings I get after reading social media.

It may be dopamine / self-help related, but no actually, I think all of that is part of the issue (discovered that in high school when it was taking off). Something about the way I'm fundamentally interacting with the medium seems so horrible and icky the more I mature.

在 HN 中查看
fnordian_slip 2026/04/12 15:41
我同意你的观点，但关于你的附录：

目前，小众爱好者论坛仍然安全。只是对石油灯笼修复没有足够的商业兴趣，不值得任何人花时间去毒害这口特别的井。

即使是一些较大的利基爱好，例如咸水水族馆社区，目前似乎也相当安全（尽管许多论坛都有成员互相访问以交易珊瑚并欣赏彼此的水族箱，这也有帮助）。

I agree with you, but as to your addendum:

Niche hobbyist forums are still safe, for now. There's just not enough commercial interest in petroleum lantern restoration to make it worth anyone's time to poison this particular well.

Even some larger niche hobbies like the saltwater aquarium community seemspretty safe for now (though it also helps that many forums have members who visit each other to trade corals and admire each others tanks).

还有代码？作品 4.6？

Is the ChatGPT launch the "low background steel" date for writing?

What's are the dates for images and video? Nano Banana Pro and Seedance 2.0?

And code? Opus 4.6?

在 HN 中查看
alex43578 2026/04/12 14:43
不是GPT推出，而是大概4、4o左右它才真正变得扎实。我也不认为视频还存在，至少对于超过 10 秒的视频来说是这样。
It's not the launch of GPT, but probably about 4 or 4o that it really became solid. I also don't think video is there just yet, at least for video over 10 seconds.
在 HN 中查看
operatingthetan 2026/04/12 14:50
如果人们可以阅读它并立即知道它生成的内容，它是否“可靠”？
Is it "solid" if people can read it and instantly know it's generated content?
在 HN 中查看
kaashif 2026/04/12 15:43
不会。但是您可以轻松制作和发布生成时不易检测到的内容。

你只会在整容手术很糟糕的时候才会注意到它，但这并不意味着所有的整容手术看起来都很糟糕......

No. But you can easily make and post content that is not easily detectable as generated.

You only notice plastic surgery when it's bad, but that doesn't mean all plastic surgery looks bad...

在 HN 中查看
postsantum 2026/04/12 14:38
4-5 个单词的句子 ted 谈话风格，是的。即使人类这样做时我也讨厌它。这就像励志演讲者尝试写小说
4-5 words sentences ted talk style, yes. I hated it even when humans were doing it. It's like motivational speakers trying their hand at writing novels
在 HN 中查看
patcon 2026/04/12 15:20
其实我很喜欢这个，也很感动。在阅读时，我的思绪快速闪过我知识库中保存的数十个个人模因（即我经常走过的思维路径）。这是一个 30mb 的文本语料库，我在其中记录了我所有的工作、同行对话和想法，以及（除其他外）我思考我的精神实践……我对复杂系统（包括道教教义）的意义建构。这篇文章基本上与我在自己认知的边缘所做的工作纠缠在一起，我正在研究我最原始、最脆弱但珍贵的想法。

我不认为这是陈词滥调，我认为其中有一些与“生命结构”（克里斯托弗·亚历山大意义上的[1]）相关的东西，并且很多东西存在于文本的边缘之外。

对于那些不喜欢这个的人，我真的很好奇：你会说你不喜欢隐喻吗？您是否倾向于感到与诗歌写作脱节并缺乏共鸣？

[1]： https://dorian.substack.com/p/at-any-given-moment-in-a-proce...

编辑：我觉得这篇文章给了我很多安静的 A，或者在给定的思维方向上可能有 A 的味道。从 A/B/U 系统的意义上来说，我在这里将其他人解释为获得 B 或 U： https://openresearchinstitute.org/onboarding/A_B_U.html

I actually loved this, and felt moved. While reading, my mind fired rapidly through dozens of personal memes (i.e. my regularly trod thought-paths) that I keep in my knowledge-base. This is the 30mb text corpus where I log all my work and peer conversations and thoughts, and (amongst other things) where I think through what I would consider my spiritual practices... my sensemaking around complex systems, including Daoist teachings. This text basically entangled itself with the work I am doing at the outer edges of my own knowing, where I am working on my rawest and most fragile but precious thoughts.

I don't think this is trite, I think there is something in this that is in contact with "living structure" (in the Christopher Alexander sense[1]), and much exists outside the edges of the text.

To those who dislike this, I am genuinely curious: Would you say you dislike metaphor? Do you tend to feel disconnected and lacking resonance with poetic writing?

[1]: https://dorian.substack.com/p/at-any-given-moment-in-a-proce...

EDIT: I experience this writing as giving me many quiet A's, or perhaps a smell of A's in a given direction of thought. I interpret others here as getting either B's or U's, in the sense of this A/B/U system: https://openresearchinstitute.org/onboarding/A_B_U.html

rukshn 2026/04/12 15:24

Why is it hacking the back button in my mobile safari browser? And why the title is different from the page?
在 HN 中查看
Phileosopher 2026/04/12 15:15
最令人惊叹的艺术实际上并不是灵感的产物，而是来自严格的编辑（或者严格的实践，如果是现场的话）。

好的写作需要 很多 的“后期制作”来敲定想法。其中大部分是删除与作者想要的内容无关的内容。

这种 LLM 趋势是一个更大的历史模式的一部分，该模式使编辑不再需要我们在大脑中思考事情：

  A. At one time, the editing was mental load, since writing was tedious.

  B. The typewriter made writing easy, but modifying it required lots of handwritten scrawling, but the mental load was still within reviewing and rewriting the content.

  C. By the end of the 20th century, editing and rewriting was a total breeze, but the mental load was still within handwritten note-taking.

  D. Once we made a bazillion forms of productivity and note-taking software, the mental load was only in thinking the thought and getting it into a computer. Everything after that was massaging the idea.

  E. Now, the regurgitation machine can get you 3/4 of the way to the finish line of your draft without even trying.
但是，我确信我们在每一次转变中都失去了一些东西。经过深思熟虑而形成的一句恰到好处的句子比 85 段废话更有力量。

保罗·格雷厄姆关于优秀写作的文章（https://paulgraham.com/goodwriting.html）将“正确的”书面想法定义为“很好地发展它们——得出最重要的结论，并以正确的细节程度探索每一个结论”。

我的观点是，信息过度时代的荒谬复杂性使得“正确”的详细程度如下：

  1. Executive summary that children and dumb people can understand.

  2. Tightly-defined specifications for everyone who cares or needs to know.

  3. Footnotes and background information that you can throw everything and the kitchen sink onto. This includes attempts to persuade, artful descriptions, feelings you had, associations to other things, and that general elegant "waxing on" that everyone gets the fancy for doing sometimes.
而且，按照这种态度，法学硕士只适合第三点。
Most amazing art isn't really a product of inspiration, but from severe editing (or severe practice, if it's live).

Good writing needs a lot of "post-production" to get the ideas hammered out. Most of it is removing content that isn't central to what the writer wants.

This LLM trend is part of a larger historical pattern that shifts editing away from us having to think things in our brain:

  A. At one time, the editing was mental load, since writing was tedious.

  B. The typewriter made writing easy, but modifying it required lots of handwritten scrawling, but the mental load was still within reviewing and rewriting the content.

  C. By the end of the 20th century, editing and rewriting was a total breeze, but the mental load was still within handwritten note-taking.

  D. Once we made a bazillion forms of productivity and note-taking software, the mental load was only in thinking the thought and getting it into a computer. Everything after that was massaging the idea.

  E. Now, the regurgitation machine can get you 3/4 of the way to the finish line of your draft without even trying.
But, I'm convinced we lost something on each of these transitions. There is more power in one well-placed sentence assembled over tremendous meditation than 85 paragraphs of slop.

Paul Graham's essay on good writing (https://paulgraham.com/goodwriting.html) defines "right" written ideas as "developing them well — drawing the conclusions that matter most, and exploring each one to the right level of detail".

My opinion is that the absurd complexities of the Over-Information Age make the "right" level of detail the following:

  1. Executive summary that children and dumb people can understand.

  2. Tightly-defined specifications for everyone who cares or needs to know.

  3. Footnotes and background information that you can throw everything and the kitchen sink onto. This includes attempts to persuade, artful descriptions, feelings you had, associations to other things, and that general elegant "waxing on" that everyone gets the fancy for doing sometimes.
And, in this attitude, LLMs are only good for #3.
在 HN 中查看

在 HN 中查看
